title: Complete Docker & Kubernetes Concepts: Beginner to Advanced

topics.description: Virtual Machines (VMs) use a Hypervisor to virtualize physical hardware, allowing multiple full Guest Operating Systems (OS) to run concurrently. This brings significant overhead since each VM requires its own full OS. Containers, conversely, virtualize the OS itself. They share the Host OS kernel and only package the application code, runtime, system tools, and libraries needed. This makes containers incredibly lightweight, allowing them to boot in milliseconds and achieve much higher server density.
topics.real_world_example: A Virtual Machine is like building completely separate standalone houses on a plot of land—each needing its own foundation and plumbing. A Container is like an apartment building: all tenants (apps) share the same underlying foundation and plumbing (the Host OS kernel), but each tenant has their own secure, locked apartment.
topics.code:
# VM provisioning takes minutes. Containers take milliseconds.
docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx:latest

practice_questions.q: Why is a container significantly faster to start up than a Virtual Machine?
practice_questions.a: It uses the host OS kernel and only runs the app process, whereas a VM must boot an entire guest OS.

practice_questions.q: Can a Linux container run natively on a bare-metal Windows OS without any virtualization? Explain why or why not.
practice_questions.a: No. Containers share the host kernel. Linux containers require a Linux kernel, so Windows uses WSL2 behind the scenes.

practice_questions.q: If an application requires modifying the core OS kernel, should you use a VM or a Container?
practice_questions.a: You must use a VM. Containers cannot modify the shared host kernel for security and isolation reasons.

topics.description: A Docker Image is an immutable (read-only) binary template containing the application and its dependencies. It is built in layers to maximize reuse. A Container is the live, runnable instance of an Image. When an Image is run, Docker adds a thin read-write layer on top of the read-only image layers, allowing the container to make temporary modifications.
topics.real_world_example: An Image is a fixed blueprint/recipe for baking a cake. You cannot eat a recipe. A Container is the actual physical cake baked using that recipe. You can bake 10 identical cakes (Containers) from a single recipe (Image).
topics.code:
# Pulling the blueprint (Image)
docker pull redis:alpine

# Creating live instances (Containers) from the blueprint
docker run -d --name cache-1 redis:alpine
docker run -d --name cache-2 redis:alpine

practice_questions.q: If you run a container, create a file inside it, and then stop and remove the container, what happens to the file?
practice_questions.a: The file is permanently deleted. Container writable layers are ephemeral.

practice_questions.q: How does Docker's layered file system optimize disk space when storing multiple images based on the same OS?
practice_questions.a: Docker shares identical read-only image layers across containers, only allocating space for the thin writable layer.

practice_questions.q: Write a command to list all currently running containers on your machine.
practice_questions.a: docker ps

topics.description: A Dockerfile is a declarative text document containing all the commands required to assemble a Docker Image. Common instructions include FROM (base image), WORKDIR (setting the active directory), COPY (moving files from host to image), RUN (executing commands during the build phase), and CMD (the default command to execute when the container starts).
topics.real_world_example: It is an automated assembly line protocol. First, take a bare car chassis (FROM ubuntu), install the seats and steering wheel (RUN apt-get install), load the custom radio (COPY radio.bin), and finally turn the ignition key when the car is sold (CMD ["start-engine"]).
topics.code:
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /usr/src/app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install --production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

practice_questions.q: What is the critical difference between the RUN instruction and the CMD instruction in a Dockerfile?
practice_questions.a: RUN executes during the image build process. CMD specifies the default command when the container starts.

practice_questions.q: Why is it a best practice to copy `package.json` and run `npm install` before copying the rest of the application source code?
practice_questions.a: Docker caches layers. By installing dependencies first, code changes won't bust the expensive npm install cache layer.

practice_questions.q: Write a Dockerfile to run a simple Python script `app.py`.
practice_questions.a:
FROM python:3.9
COPY app.py .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]

topics.description: A Docker Registry is a highly scalable server side application that stores and lets you distribute Docker images. Docker Hub is the default public registry provided by Docker Inc. Enterprise environments usually host private registries (e.g., AWS ECR, Azure ACR, JFrog Artifactory) to securely store proprietary code.
topics.real_world_example: Think of it as an App Store for servers. Instead of downloading applications to your smartphone from the Apple App Store, servers download ready-to-run database and web server images from a Docker Registry.
topics.code:
# Tagging an image for a specific remote registry repository
docker tag my-local-app:v1.0 myorg/my-app:v1.0

# Pushing the image to the remote registry
docker push myorg/my-app:v1.0

practice_questions.q: If you run `docker run postgres` and the postgres image is not on your local machine, what exactly does Docker do?
practice_questions.a: It automatically pulls the postgres image from Docker Hub, then creates and starts the container.

practice_questions.q: Why do companies prefer using private registries like AWS ECR instead of Docker Hub for their internal applications?
practice_questions.a: For enhanced security, access control, and keeping proprietary code images off public networks.

practice_questions.q: How do you authenticate your Docker CLI client with a remote private registry?
practice_questions.a: Using 'docker login <registry-url>' and providing your credentials.

topics.description: By default, containers are highly isolated. Port mapping (`-p host_port:container_port`) punches a hole in this isolation, linking a port on the host machine to a port inside the container network. Environment variables (`-e KEY=VALUE`) inject dynamic configuration settings into the container at runtime without hardcoding them into the image.
topics.real_world_example: Port mapping is like setting up call forwarding from your company's main phone line (host) to your specific desk phone (container). Environment variables are like handing the worker a sealed envelope with today's secret database password just as they start their shift.
topics.code:
# Run a MySQL container
# -p exposes it to the outside world
# -e configures the root password dynamically
docker run -d -p 3306:3306 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=supersecret mysql:8.0

practice_questions.q: If you run a web server container on port 80 without the `-p` flag, can you access it from your host machine's web browser? Why?
practice_questions.a: No, you must explicitly publish ports using -p to map the host port to the container port.

practice_questions.q: Why is it dangerous to hardcode database credentials inside a Dockerfile using the ENV instruction?
practice_questions.a: Anyone with access to the image can easily inspect the layers or run 'docker inspect' to see the plaintext secrets.

practice_questions.q: Write a Docker command to run an Nginx container mapping host port 8080 to container port 80.
practice_questions.a: docker run -p 8080:80 nginx

topics.description: Containers are ephemeral; if a container is deleted, the read-write layer is destroyed and all data is permanently lost. Docker Volumes solve this by mounting a directory from the Host OS directly into the container's file system, allowing data to persist across container lifecycles.
topics.real_world_example: Saving your important essay on an external USB flash drive (Volume) rather than the computer's temporary RAM. If the computer crashes or gets replaced (Container deleted), you just plug the USB drive into the new computer and all your data is instantly there.
topics.code:
# Create a named volume managed by Docker
docker volume create my_db_data

# Mount the volume into the container's data directory
docker run -d -v my_db_data:/var/lib/mysql -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret mysql:8.0

practice_questions.q: What is the difference between a 'Bind Mount' and a 'Named Volume' in Docker?
practice_questions.a: A Bind Mount links a specific host path. A Named Volume is managed entirely by Docker internally.

practice_questions.q: If you delete a container that was writing data to a Docker Volume, what happens to the Volume and its data?
practice_questions.a: The Volume and its data persist safely on the host machine until explicitly deleted.

practice_questions.q: Why should you never store relational database data solely inside a container's writable layer?
practice_questions.a: Because the writable layer is deleted when the container is removed, leading to total data loss.

topics.description: Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. By using a declarative YAML file, you can configure application services, networks, and volumes in one place. A single command (`docker-compose up`) spins up the entire integrated environment.
topics.real_world_example: A symphony conductor's master sheet music. Instead of running around and telling the drummer, the violinist, and the flutist individually what to do and when to start, the compose file gives the exact instructions for the entire orchestra to start playing perfectly in sync.
topics.code:
version: '3.8'
services:
  frontend:
    image: react-app:latest
    ports: ["3000:80"]
    depends_on: [backend]
  backend:
    image: node-api:latest
    environment:
      - DB_HOST=database
  database:
    image: postgres:14
    volumes: [pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data]

volumes:
  pg_data:

practice_questions.q: What does the `depends_on` directive do in a docker-compose.yml file?
practice_questions.a: It dictates the startup order of services, ensuring dependencies start first.

practice_questions.q: How does Docker Compose handle networking between the services defined in the YAML file?
practice_questions.a: It automatically creates a custom bridge network and attaches all services to it, allowing DNS resolution by service name.

practice_questions.q: Write a command to stop and completely remove all containers, networks, and volumes created by `docker-compose up`.
practice_questions.a: docker-compose down -v

topics.description: Docker networking allows containers to communicate securely. The default 'bridge' network connects containers on the same host. The 'host' network removes network isolation entirely. The 'overlay' network facilitates communication between containers running on completely different Docker daemon hosts (used heavily in Docker Swarm).
topics.real_world_example: The 'bridge' network is like a private office intercom system where employees can talk to each other but the outside world cannot hear. The 'host' network is like shouting into the public street. The 'overlay' network is a secure VPN connecting offices in New York and London.
topics.code:
# Creating a custom bridge network
docker network create secure-backend-net

# Attaching containers to the custom network for automatic DNS resolution
docker run -d --name db --network secure-backend-net postgres
docker run -d --name api --network secure-backend-net my-api

practice_questions.q: If two containers are on the default 'bridge' network, can they ping each other by their container names? Why or why not?
practice_questions.a: No. The default bridge does not provide automatic DNS resolution. Custom bridge networks are required for name resolution.

practice_questions.q: What are the security implications of running a container with `--network host`?
practice_questions.a: The container bypasses network isolation and binds directly to the host's network interfaces, reducing security.

practice_questions.q: How does Docker implement internal DNS resolution for custom bridge networks?
practice_questions.a: Docker embeds a DNS server at 127.0.0.11 inside custom networks to resolve container names to IPs.

topics.description: Multi-stage builds allow you to use multiple `FROM` statements in a single Dockerfile. You can compile your application in a heavy 'builder' stage containing all compilers and SDKs, and then copy ONLY the final compiled binary into a lightweight 'runtime' stage. This dramatically reduces image size and attack surface.
topics.real_world_example: A restaurant kitchen process. Stage 1 is the messy preparation area filled with peelers, bulk ingredients, and raw flour (build tools). Stage 2 is the clean dining room. You don't bring the messy peelers into the dining room; you only bring the finished, plated meal (the compiled binary).
topics.code:
# STAGE 1: Build
FROM golang:1.20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN go build -o myapp main.go

# STAGE 2: Minimal Runtime
FROM alpine:latest
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /src/myapp .
CMD ["./myapp"]

practice_questions.q: Why is reducing the final Docker image size important for production environments?
practice_questions.a: It speeds up deployment pulls, reduces storage costs, and minimizes the attack surface.

practice_questions.q: How does a multi-stage build improve the security of your final container image?
practice_questions.a: By compiling the app in one stage and copying only the compiled binary to a minimal runtime stage, leaving build tools behind.

practice_questions.q: Can you have more than two stages in a multi-stage Dockerfile?
practice_questions.a: Yes, you can have as many stages as needed, copying artifacts between any of them.

topics.description: Kubernetes is a container orchestration engine. It operates on a Master-Worker architecture. The Control Plane (Master) manages cluster state via components like the API Server (frontend), Scheduler (assigns work), Controller Manager (maintains state), and etcd (key-value data store). Worker Nodes actually run the applications using the Kubelet (node agent), Kube-proxy (networking), and a Container Runtime (containerd).
topics.real_world_example: A massive construction site. The Control Plane is the team of architects and foremen in the trailer making decisions, reading blueprints, and assigning tasks. The Worker Nodes are the laborers, cranes, and trucks out in the field doing the actual heavy lifting.
topics.code:
# Check the status of all nodes in the cluster
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# Output will show the Control-Plane nodes and Worker nodes
# NAME         STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
# k8s-master   Ready    control-plane   12d   v1.27.0
# k8s-node1    Ready    <none>          12d   v1.27.0

practice_questions.q: If the Kubernetes Control Plane goes down, do the existing running applications on the Worker Nodes immediately crash? Explain.
practice_questions.a: No, running Pods continue to function, but you cannot deploy new Pods or change cluster state until the Control Plane recovers.

practice_questions.q: What is the purpose of the `etcd` component in the Kubernetes architecture?
practice_questions.a: It is the highly available key-value store that acts as the single source of truth for the cluster's state.

practice_questions.q: What role does the `kubelet` play on a worker node?
practice_questions.a: It is the primary node agent that communicates with the API server and ensures containers are running in Pods.

topics.description: Kubernetes does not run containers directly; it encapsulates them in an abstraction called a Pod. A Pod represents a single instance of a running process. It can hold one or multiple tightly coupled containers. Containers within the same Pod share the exact same network namespace (IP address and port space) and can share storage volumes.
topics.real_world_example: Think of a Pod as a literal pod of peas. The pod is the Kubernetes wrapper, and the peas inside are the Docker containers. Usually, there is just one pea (application container) per pod, but sometimes you have a 'helper' pea (like a logging sidecar) sharing the same pod environment.
topics.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-web-pod
  labels:
    tier: frontend
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80

practice_questions.q: If a Pod contains two containers, how do those two containers communicate with each other over the network?
practice_questions.a: They share the same network namespace and communicate via 'localhost'.

practice_questions.q: Why is it generally considered bad practice to put a Database container and a Web Server container in the exact same Pod?
practice_questions.a: It tightly couples them, forcing them to scale together and preventing independent updates, defeating microservices architecture.

practice_questions.q: What happens to a Pod's IP address if the Pod crashes and is restarted by the cluster?
practice_questions.a: The new Pod gets a completely new, random IP address.

topics.description: A ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at all times, providing self-healing. A Deployment is a higher-level abstraction that manages ReplicaSets, enabling declarative updates, rolling rollouts, and easy rollbacks of application versions without downtime.
topics.real_world_example: A Deployment is like a meticulous fast-food store manager. You give the manager an instruction: "I always need exactly 3 cashiers working." If a cashier quits (a Pod crashes), the manager immediately hires a replacement. If you want to update the uniforms (deploy a new image version), the manager rotates the cashiers one by one so the store never closes.
topics.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template: # This is the Pod template
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: mycompany/api:v2

practice_questions.q: How does a rolling update strategy in a Deployment prevent application downtime?
practice_questions.a: It gradually replaces old Pods with new Pods, ensuring a minimum number of healthy Pods are always serving traffic.

practice_questions.q: What is the command to rollback a Deployment to its previous version if a bug is discovered?
practice_questions.a: kubectl rollout undo deployment <deployment-name>

practice_questions.q: If you manually delete a Pod that is managed by a Deployment, what immediately happens next?
practice_questions.a: The Deployment's ReplicaSet notices the count dropped and instantly spins up a new Pod to replace it.

topics.description: Because Pods are ephemeral and their IP addresses change constantly when rescheduled, direct Pod-to-Pod communication is fragile. A Service provides a stable, static IP address and DNS name that acts as an internal load balancer, routing traffic to dynamic Pods based on Label Selectors. Types include ClusterIP (internal only), NodePort (exposes port on every node), and LoadBalancer (provisions cloud load balancer).
topics.real_world_example: A company's 1-800 customer support hotline. The customer dials the single static 1-800 number (Service IP). Behind the scenes, the call system routes the call to whichever specific support agent (Pod) is currently at their desk, abstracting away the fact that agent locations constantly change.
topics.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: backend-svc
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: backend # Routes traffic to Pods with this label
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

practice_questions.q: Explain the difference between a `ClusterIP` service and a `NodePort` service.
practice_questions.a: ClusterIP is internal only. NodePort opens a specific port on every Worker Node's IP to expose the service externally.

practice_questions.q: How does a Service know exactly which Pods it should route traffic to?
practice_questions.a: By using Label Selectors. It watches for Pods that match the labels defined in the Service specification.

practice_questions.q: If a Service is of type `LoadBalancer` and you deploy it on bare-metal (not in AWS/GCP/Azure), what usually happens?
practice_questions.a: It usually stays in a 'Pending' state unless you install a bare-metal load balancer like MetalLB.

topics.description: Kubernetes allows decoupling configuration artifacts from application code. ConfigMaps hold plain-text key-value pairs (like URLs or application properties) and can be injected as Environment Variables or mounted as files. Secrets serve the same purpose but are specifically for sensitive data (API keys, passwords, TLS certs) and are Base64 encoded and stored securely in etcd.
topics.real_world_example: A ConfigMap is the public employee handbook available on the company intranet detailing standard operating procedures. A Secret is the combination to the physical safe in the manager's office—highly restricted and obfuscated from the general public.
topics.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
type: Opaque
data:
  # Values must be base64 encoded
  username: YWRtaW4=
  password: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXQ=

practice_questions.q: Is Base64 encoding in a Kubernetes Secret the same thing as encryption? Explain.
practice_questions.a: No. Base64 is encoding, not encryption. It can be easily decoded by anyone who has access to it.

practice_questions.q: How can you pass a value from a ConfigMap as an environment variable into a Pod?
practice_questions.a: Using the 'envFrom' field or 'valueFrom: configMapKeyRef' in the Pod specification.

practice_questions.q: If you update a ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume in a running Pod, does the Pod automatically see the new values?
practice_questions.a: Yes, kubelet periodically syncs the volume, but the application must know how to reload the file without restarting.

topics.description: While Services manage internal L4 load balancing, an Ingress provides L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) routing. It acts as a smart router that exposes HTTP and HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services within the cluster. It can handle path-based routing, hostname-based virtual hosting, and SSL/TLS termination.
topics.real_world_example: A traffic cop at a massive intersection leading to different office parks. If a car's destination says 'api.company.com', the cop directs them down the left highway. If the car wants 'shop.company.com/checkout', the cop directs them down the right highway.
topics.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: main-routing
spec:
  rules:
  - host: api.myapp.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /v1
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-service
            port:
              number: 80

practice_questions.q: Why would you use an Ingress resource instead of just creating multiple LoadBalancer services?
practice_questions.a: Ingress provides URL path-based routing and SSL termination using a single external IP, saving cloud load balancer costs.

practice_questions.q: What is an Ingress Controller, and why doesn't an Ingress resource work without one?
practice_questions.a: An Ingress Controller (like NGINX) is the actual reverse proxy software that implements the rules defined in the Ingress resource.

practice_questions.q: How do you configure SSL/TLS termination inside an Ingress resource?
practice_questions.a: By defining a 'tls' section with a 'secretName' containing the SSL certificate and private key.

topics.description: Namespaces provide logical separation and isolation of resources within a single physical Kubernetes cluster. They are crucial for multi-tenant environments. Resource Quotas can be applied per namespace to strictly enforce hard limits on the amount of CPU, Memory, and total Pods that namespace can consume.
topics.real_world_example: Imagine dividing a massive physical warehouse (the Cluster) into separate locked sections (Namespaces) named 'Development', 'Testing', and 'Production'. Resource Quotas ensure that the 'Development' section cannot accidentally hog all the electricity and space, ensuring 'Production' remains stable.
topics.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-limits
  namespace: development
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"

practice_questions.q: Can a Pod in the 'development' namespace communicate with a Pod in the 'production' namespace by default?
practice_questions.a: Yes, unless NetworkPolicies are explicitly configured to block cross-namespace traffic.

practice_questions.q: If a namespace has a ResourceQuota applied, what happens if a user tries to deploy a Pod without specifying CPU/Memory requests?
practice_questions.a: The Kubernetes API server will reject the Pod creation request.

practice_questions.q: What is the command to switch your default `kubectl` context to a specific namespace?
practice_questions.a: kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=<namespace-name>

topics.description: Kubernetes abstracts storage provisioning. A Persistent Volume (PV) is a physical piece of storage in the cluster (e.g., an AWS EBS volume or NFS share) provisioned by an admin. A Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) is a request made by a developer/Pod to consume a specific amount of that storage. This decouples storage infrastructure from application deployment.
topics.real_world_example: A PV is like the massive 100TB physical SAN array sitting in the server room, purchased by the IT admin. A PVC is a ticket submitted by a developer saying, "I need a 50GB slice of fast SSD storage for my database project." Kubernetes matches the ticket to available storage.
topics.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: mysql-storage-claim
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 20Gi

practice_questions.q: What does the `ReadWriteOnce` access mode signify for a Persistent Volume?
practice_questions.a: It means the volume can be mounted as read-write by a single node at a time.

practice_questions.q: Explain the difference between static provisioning of PVs and dynamic provisioning using StorageClasses.
practice_questions.a: Static involves an admin manually creating PVs. Dynamic uses a StorageClass to automatically provision cloud storage when a PVC requests it.

practice_questions.q: If a PVC is deleted, what determines whether the underlying PV data is deleted or retained?
practice_questions.a: The 'persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy' of the PV (either Retain, Delete, or Recycle).

topics.description: Unlike Deployments which assume applications are stateless and interchangeable, StatefulSets are designed specifically for stateful applications like Databases (MongoDB, Kafka, Zookeeper). They provide strict guarantees about ordering, graceful scaling, stable persistent storage (via VolumeClaimTemplates), and unique, stable network identifiers (e.g., db-0, db-1).
topics.real_world_example: Deployments are treated like cattle: if one gets sick, you replace it with an identical, unnamed cow. StatefulSets are treated like pets: you name them individually (Fido, Fluffy), you care for their specific needs, and if Fido goes to the vet, you wait for exactly Fido to come back.
topics.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: kafka
spec:
  serviceName: "kafka-headless"
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: kafka
  # Guarantees pods are named kafka-0, kafka-1, kafka-2 in order

practice_questions.q: Why is a 'Headless Service' strictly required when deploying a StatefulSet?
practice_questions.a: To provide stable, unique network identifiers (DNS records) for each individual Pod in the StatefulSet.

practice_questions.q: During a scale-down event in a StatefulSet with 3 replicas (0, 1, 2), which pod is terminated first?
practice_questions.a: The Pod with the highest ordinal index (in this case, pod-2).

practice_questions.q: How do VolumeClaimTemplates ensure data safety in a StatefulSet compared to a regular Deployment?
practice_questions.a: They provision a unique, dedicated PersistentVolumeClaim for every single replica in the StatefulSet.

topics.description: A DaemonSet ensures that exactly ONE copy of a specific Pod runs on ALL (or a selected subset of) Nodes in the cluster. This is essential for cluster-wide infrastructure tasks like log collection or monitoring. Jobs run a Pod to completion (execute a script, then terminate) rather than keeping it running forever. CronJobs run Jobs on a scheduled time basis.
topics.real_world_example: A DaemonSet is like a security guard stationed at every single entrance of a sprawling corporate campus. A Job is like a hired cleaner who comes in once, scrubs the floors, and goes home. A CronJob is that same cleaner scheduled to come exactly at 2 AM every Sunday.
topics.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: fluentd-log-collector
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      name: fluentd
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        name: fluentd

practice_questions.q: If you add a brand new Worker Node to a Kubernetes cluster, what does a DaemonSet automatically do?
practice_questions.a: It automatically detects the new node and schedules exactly one copy of the DaemonSet Pod onto it.

practice_questions.q: How does a Job differ from a standard Deployment regarding Pod restart policies?
practice_questions.a: A Job runs a task to completion and stops. A Deployment keeps Pods running continuously.

practice_questions.q: Write a CronJob schedule expression to run a task at midnight every Friday.

topics.description: The HPA automatically scales the number of Pod replicas in a Deployment or StatefulSet up or down based on observed metrics like CPU utilization, Memory usage, or custom external metrics (like queue length). It acts as an automated feedback loop for application performance.
topics.real_world_example: A smart checkout system at a supermarket. Normally, 2 cashiers are working. When sensors detect the store is getting incredibly crowded (CPU spikes > 80%), the system automatically calls 5 more cashiers (Pods) to the front. As the store empties out, it sends them back to save money.
topics.code:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-frontend-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-frontend
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 75

practice_questions.q: Why is it absolutely necessary for Pods to have CPU requests configured for the HPA to work?
practice_questions.a: The HPA calculates scaling metrics based on CPU utilization percentages, which requires a defined baseline request.

practice_questions.q: What is the difference between the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and the Cluster Autoscaler?
practice_questions.a: HPA adds more Pods to nodes. Cluster Autoscaler adds more physical Worker Nodes to the cloud provider.

practice_questions.q: How can you configure an HPA to scale based on the number of HTTP requests per second?
practice_questions.a: By using Custom Metrics APIs connected to monitoring systems like Prometheus.

topics.description: Helm is the official package manager for Kubernetes. Instead of managing dozens of complex, hardcoded YAML files, Helm uses 'Charts'. A Chart is a collection of templated YAML files where values can be injected dynamically, allowing you to deploy complex applications with a single command.
topics.real_world_example: Helm is to Kubernetes what `apt-get` is to Ubuntu or `npm` is to Node.js. Instead of downloading and compiling a database from scratch using 20 different config files, you just type `helm install database` and it magically sets everything up for you.
topics.code:
# Add a public chart repository
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami

# Install a complex distributed Redis cluster with a single command
helm install my-redis bitnami/redis

# Override default template values dynamically
helm upgrade my-redis bitnami/redis --set auth.password=NewSecret123

practice_questions.q: What is the function of the `values.yaml` file in a Helm Chart?
practice_questions.a: It stores the default configuration variables that are injected into the Helm Chart templates.

practice_questions.q: How does Helm keep track of previous deployments to allow for simple rollbacks?
practice_questions.a: It saves each deployment as a 'Release' revision in Kubernetes Secrets, allowing instant rollbacks using 'helm rollback'.

practice_questions.q: What command would you use to view all the variables that can be customized in a specific Helm Chart before installing it?
practice_questions.a: helm show values <chart-name>

topics.description: RBAC is the mechanism for securing the Kubernetes API. You define permissions using Roles (scoped to a namespace) or ClusterRoles (cluster-wide). You then assign these permissions to Users, Groups, or ServiceAccounts (identities for Pods) using RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings.
topics.real_world_example: An electronic keycard system in a high-security facility. A junior developer's keycard (ServiceAccount) might only have a RoleBinding that lets them view the 'Dev Lab' (namespace). The IT Admin's keycard has a ClusterRoleBinding that allows them to unlock every single door in the entire facility.
topics.code:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: dev-team
  name: pod-reader-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""] # Core API group
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch", "list"] # Read-only permissions

practice_questions.q: What is the difference between a `RoleBinding` and a `ClusterRoleBinding`?
practice_questions.a: A RoleBinding grants permissions within a specific namespace. A ClusterRoleBinding grants permissions cluster-wide.

practice_questions.q: Why is it dangerous to attach a ServiceAccount with Cluster-Admin privileges to a web-facing Pod?
practice_questions.a: If the Pod is compromised, the attacker gains full control over the entire Kubernetes cluster.

practice_questions.q: If a User has no RoleBindings assigned to them in Kubernetes, what actions are they allowed to perform by default?
practice_questions.a: Zero actions. Kubernetes uses strict Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with default-deny.

key_themes: Advanced state management in distributed systems
Cluster security and API access control
